content.text: There was once a poor miller who died, leaving only his mill to his eldest son, his donkey to the second, and his cat to the youngest.
The youngest son was very sad and said, "My brothers can work together to make a living, but once I have eaten my cat, I shall die of hunger."
The cat, who was listening, looked up and said, "Do not worry, my master. Just give me a bag and a pair of boots so I can walk through the brambles, and you will see that your inheritance
is not as bad as you think." Though the young man had little hope, he spent his last coins on a small pair of leather boots and a sturdy sack.
The cat pulled on the boots, slung the bag over his shoulder, and set off into the forest. Before long, the clever cat caught a fat rabbit in his bag.
He took it straight to the royal palace and asked to speak with the King. Bowing low, the cat said, "Sire, I bring you this rabbit as a gift from my master, the Marquis of Carabas"—for
that was the grand name the cat had invented for his master. The King was delighted and thanked the cat. Over the next few weeks, the cat continued to bring gifts of wild game to the
palace, always presenting them on behalf of the Marquis of Carabas. One day, the cat learned that the King was going to drive along the riverbank with his beautiful daughter, the
Princess. The cat ran to his master and said, "If you do as I say, your fortune is made. Go and bathe in the river at the place I show you, and leave the rest to me."
The young man did as he was told. While he was swimming, the King’s carriage approached. The cat began to shout with all his might, "Help! Help! The Marquis of Carabas is drowning!"
The King recognized the cat who had brought him so many fine gifts and ordered his guards to rescue the Marquis.
The cat then told the King that thieves had stolen his master's clothes while he was bathing (though the cat had actually hidden them behind a large rock).
The King immediately ordered his servants to fetch one of his finest suits of clothes for the young man. Dressed in royal garments, the miller’s son looked incredibly handsome.
The Princess fell in love with him at once, and the King invited him to join them in the carriage. The cat, overjoyed at his success, ran ahead of the carriage.
He came to a vast meadow where farmers were mowing grass. He said to them, "If you do not tell the King that this meadow belongs to the Marquis of Carabas, you will all be chopped as
fine as mincemeat!" When the King’s carriage passed by, the King asked who owned the beautiful meadow. "The Marquis of Carabas, Sire!" the terrified farmers replied in unison.
The King was highly impressed. The cat ran further ahead and came to a grand castle owned by a wealthy ogre, who was the real master of all the surrounding lands.
The cat knocked on the gate and asked to speak with the ogre, pretending to pay his respects. "I have been told," said the cat, "that you possess the marvelous power to
transform yourself into any creature—even a lion or an elephant!" "That is true!" roared the proud ogre, and he instantly turned into a giant, roaring lion.
The cat was terrified and scrambled up to the roof, waiting until the ogre changed back into his human form. "That was incredible!" gasped the cat, catching his breath.
"But surely it is impossible for someone as large as you to turn into a very small animal, like a rat or a mouse?" "Impossible?" bellowed the ogre. "Watch this!"
Instantly, the ogre transformed into a tiny mouse, scurrying across the floor. In a flash, the clever cat pounced on the mouse and ate him right up.
Just then, the King’s carriage arrived at the castle gates. The cat ran down to welcome them. "Welcome, Your Majesty, to the castle of the Marquis of Carabas!"
The King was amazed by the grand castle and the vast wealth of the Marquis. A magnificent feast was prepared, and that very day, the King offered the Marquis the hand of his
daughter in marriage. The miller’s son gladly accepted, and the cat became a great lord, who never had to hunt for mice again, except sometimes for his own amusement.
